metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a covering AC (or extend AC4) that verifies the harness raises an error or sets an explicit `validity_guard_failed: true` flag in e2_baseline.json when any class exceeds the 20% INDETERMINATE cap, so downstream R-row consumers can programmatically reject invalid runs.
evidence: "a run is INVALID and must be re-run if >20% of a class's mutants OR >20% of its controls return verdict INDETERMINATE"
AC4 prove step checks only field presence and vocabulary: "assert set(b)=={'1','2','3','4','5'} and all(req<=set(b[k]) for k in b)" — no guard-enforcement check
No AC requires harness to raise/exit-nonzero or set a `validity_guard_failed` flag when the cap is breached
findings.finding: The run-validity guard (>20% INDETERMINATE triggers invalidation requiring a re-run) is described in ## What but no AC verifies that the harness actually ENFORCES the guard — i.e., that it refuses to write or marks invalid `e2_baseline.json` results when the cap is exceeded. AC4's verify script only checks that fields exist and vocabulary is correct, not that a validity-guard breach blocks `meets_threshold` from being set True.
findings.location: ## What (AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling, run-validity guard) vs AC4 (run_e2_benchmark.py)
scenarios: Harness silently records results with 30% INDETERMINATEs and sets meets_threshold=False (or True), making the guard purely documentary rather than enforced — R1/R3/R4 consume invalid numbers.
findings.suggested_fix: In e2_baseline.json, add a top-level `run_valid: bool` field and a per-class `validity_guard_breached: bool`. Extend AC4's prove step to assert `b[k].get('validity_guard_breached', False) == False` for all classes in the committed baseline, and document that a breached run must not be committed as the final baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and fix how `run_e2_benchmark.py` splits the ticket-store root (tmp store) from the code-grounding root (real checkout): probe `gate_source.gate_read_root` + `resolve_code_root` to confirm that passing `repo_root=<tmp-store>` with `source='local'` does NOT route code grounding to the real in-place checkout — then spec the harness to pass `repo_root=<real-checkout>` for code grounding while directing ticket reads to the tmp store via a separate mechanism (e.g. passing a custom `LLMConfig` with `tickets_path=<tmp-store>`).
evidence: Plan text: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
gate_source.py:104-117: `gate_read_root` in local mode (`else: yield`) does NOT activate `use_tickets_root` or `use_code_root`

orchestrator.py:195-203: `PlanContext.repo_root = resolve_code_root(repo_root, cfg_repo_path=..., allow_checkout_fallback=False)` — if `repo_root=<tmp-store>` is truthy, it wins the cascade (step 1) and points code-grounding at the tmp store, not the real checkout
config.py:97-98: `if repo_root: return str(repo_root)` — the tmp-store path would be returned as the code root
The tmp store (created by `rebar init` under `tempfile.mkdtemp()`) contains only rebar scaffolding, not the repo source files that agentic criteria grep/read
findings.finding: The plan asserts that calling `review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source='local', sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)` causes 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout.' This is false per the codebase. In local mode, `gate_source.gate_read_root` does not activate `use_tickets_root` or `use_code_root` (it yields bare at line 116-117). Ticket reads in `_assemble_context_uncached` use `repo_root=repo_root` (the tmp store — correct). But the code-grounding root (`PlanContext.repo_root`) is also resolved via `resolve_code_root(repo_root, cfg_repo_path=cfg.repo_path, allow_checkout_fallback=False)` — and since `repo_root=<tmp-store>` is truthy it wins the cascade at step 1, rooting code-grounding at the tmp store too. The tmp store is an empty `rebar init` directory with no source files, so agentic criteria (E4, G1G2, G6) would read an empty codebase — crippling the class-1/4 detection measurements the benchmark is designed to produce.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
scenarios: E4/G1G2/G6 agentic criteria attempt to grep/read source files from the tmp store directory, find nothing, and abstain — causing spuriously high INDETERMINATE rates that trigger the run-validity guard (>20% abstain) or silently under-count detections for class 1 (absent asserted capability) and class 4 (unnecessary mechanism)
The baseline table records near-zero detection for agentic classes (1, 4) not because the criterion is weak but because code grounding was rooted at an empty directory, producing a false baseline
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, do not pass `repo_root=<tmp-store>` to `review_plan`. Instead pass `repo_root=<real-checkout>` (the actual source tree) and direct ticket reads to the tmp store by constructing a `LLMConfig` with `tickets_path=<tmp-store-tracker-dir>` and using `use_tickets_root(<tmp-store>)` context — mirroring how `opcert_service/jobs.py` isolates ticket writes while keeping code reads on the real checkout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s scope the exact write API used to populate the throwaway store (e.g., `create_ticket` + `edit_ticket` + `link` for parent–child wiring and `depends_on` edges), and add a schema note to the mutant corpus row documenting which fields map to which rebar write calls — especially how class-2/3 `mutation_op` (re-sliced children / deleted ordering edge) is expressed as store operations.
evidence: Plan: 'Materializes each mutant AND its control (plus children) as tickets in an ISOLATED throwaway rebar store (`rebar init` under `tempfile.mkdtemp()`, `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`)'
Plan: corpus rows carry `plan_text`, `children`, `mutation_op`, `mutation_diff` — no event-stream schema

Code: `orchestrator._assemble_context_uncached` calls `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root)` then `_reads.list_tickets(parent=canonical, repo_root=repo_root)` — both are live event-store reads
Code: `_reads.show_ticket` → `tracker_dir(repo_root)` → looks for `.tickets-tracker` under the given root
Code: class-2/3 ordering-edge mutations require `depends_on`/`blocks` edges to be present and absent in the store respectively — only the API path (`create_ticket` + `link`) sets these correctly
findings.finding: The mechanism for populating synthetic mutant/control tickets into the throwaway rebar store is unspecified: the plan describes `rebar init` under a tempdir and `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`, but `review_plan(repo_root=<tmp-store>)` calls `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=<tmp-store>)` which does live event-store reads from `.tickets-tracker` under that root. The mutant corpus rows carry `plan_text`, `children`, and `mutation_diff` but not a rebar event stream, so the implementer cannot know which write API bridges from corpus to store without a spec. Valid options differ non-trivially in complexity and fidelity: `create_ticket` + `edit_ticket` (API path, acquires write locks, preserves schema), `import_tickets` (event-replay import, handles parent-child wiring), or raw event file injection (bypasses locking, fragile). The choice determines whether child tickets and `depends_on` edges (needed for class-2/3 ordering detection by G4) are correctly wired.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; also implicitly ## What (corpus schema)
scenarios: Implementer uses raw file injection, skipping `depends_on` edge wiring: class-3 mutants (consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge) cannot be meaningfully distinguished from controls because neither has the edge in the store.

Implementer uses `import_tickets` but mutant corpus rows lack the required `ticket_type` field: import silently skips rows, benchmark runs with 0 tickets, `review_plan` raises `RebarError` on every row.

Implementer uses `create_ticket` but does not issue `link` calls for `depends_on` edges: G4 (consumed-artifact-ordering) cannot fire, class-3 detection is systematically 0%, invalidating the baseline.
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s description add: 'Populates each pair via `rebar.create_ticket` (parent) + `rebar.create_ticket` (each child) + `rebar.edit_ticket` (description=plan_text) + `rebar.link` (depends_on edges from `children[].deps`); class-3 mutants omit the specific ordering link named in `mutation_diff`. Corpus rows add a `store_ops` sub-field listing the link edges to create/omit per role.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document how `review_plan` separates the CODE read root from the TICKET read root in `source='local'` mode, or switch to a dual-root invocation pattern (e.g., passing `repo_root=<real-checkout>` for code grounding while routing ticket reads through a separate tickets_path/ContextVar, mirroring how `eval_solver.case_store()` and `opcert_service/workspace.py` handle this separation).
evidence: Plan quote: 'rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source="local", sign=False, emit_sidecar=False) (__init__.py:387; ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout)'

gate_source.py:105-117: with source='local', gate_read_root only enters gate_session() with NO use_code_root/use_tickets_root context managers activated — repo_root is not split into separate code vs ticket roots

orchestrator.py:164-211: _assemble_context_uncached passes the single repo_root to both _reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=repo_root) and resolve_code_root(repo_root, ...) — there is no dual-root mechanism in local mode

The tmp store created by rebar init under tempfile.mkdtemp() contains ticket state but no Python/source files, so P2 (file existence), P3 (dependency), and agentic grounding criteria would abstain on all mutants AND controls alike
findings.finding: The plan asserts that calling `review_plan(ticket_id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source='local', ...)` will route ticket reads to the tmp store while 'code grounding reads the real in-place checkout.' This is unverified and appears incorrect: with source='local', gate_source does NOT set a separate code root context variable — `repo_root` is passed to both `_assemble_context_uncached` (which passes it to `resolve_code_root` for code grounding AND to `_reads.show_ticket` for ticket reads). A tmp-store containing materialized tickets but no source code would cause all code-grounding criteria (P2 file-reference checks, P3 dependency checks, and the agentic E4/G6/A1 criteria) to silently degrade or abstain, producing non-production-faithful reviews that undercount defect detection.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py description: '(ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout)'
scenarios: Agentic criteria E4, G6, A1 that check whether named modules/capabilities exist would return INDETERMINATE on every pair (no source to ground against), artificially inflating the INDETERMINATE rate and potentially triggering the 20% run-validity guard for the very classes (1, 4) E2 needs most
The benchmark would measure the gate running blind on code-grounding criteria, producing a non-representative baseline that underestimates what the gate would detect in production
findings.suggested_fix: Use `source='attested'` with a local snapshot, OR — since attested requires a real SHA — seed the tmp store as a git repo that symlinks/copies the real source tree for code grounding while containing the fabricated tickets. Alternatively, confirm whether passing `tickets_path=<tmp-store>` as a config override (as `apply_handle` does in attested mode) would route ticket reads separately while `repo_root=<real-checkout>` grounds code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `validity_guard_breached: bool` (per-class) and `run_valid: bool` (top-level) to the `e2_baseline.json` schema, and extend AC4's prove step to assert `all(not b[k].get('validity_guard_breached', False) for k in b)` so a breached run is machine-detectable and the committed baseline is proven valid.
evidence: 'a run is INVALID and must be re-run if >20% of a class's mutants OR >20% of its controls return verdict INDETERMINATE (2.5× the 8% historical base rate). The guard and the raw verdict distribution are recorded so abstains are auditable, not hidden.'

AC4 prove script: `assert set(b)=={'1','2','3','4','5'} and all(req<=set(b[k]) for k in b)` — checks only that field names exist, not that a validity-guard breach is flagged or that `meets_threshold` is gated on guard status

The `e2_baseline.json` schema lists `n_mutants, n_controls, detected, detection_rate, blocking_fp, fp_rate, indeterminate_mutants, indeterminate_controls, meets_threshold` — no `validity_guard_breached` or `run_valid` field
The plan says 'must be re-run' but provides no machine-readable state that would tell a downstream consumer whether the committed results are from a valid run
findings.finding: The run-validity guard ('a run is INVALID and must be re-run if >20% of a class's mutants OR controls return INDETERMINATE') is a decision rule specified in ## What, but no output state is defined for a breached run — no `validity_guard_breached` field in `e2_baseline.json`, no exit-code behavior, and AC4's prove step only checks field presence, not guard enforcement.
findings.location: ## What — 'Run-validity guard' sub-section; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (`run_e2_benchmark.py` prove step); ## Scope — `e2_baseline.json` schema
scenarios: Harness completes with 25% INDETERMINATEs in class 2; `meets_threshold` is set False but no guard flag is set; R3 consumer reads `meets_threshold=False` and interprets it as 'current gate doesn't detect layer-cake' rather than 'run was invalid'
A re-run is never triggered because the guard breach is only prose-documented, not machine-enforced or machine-readable in the output artifact
findings.suggested_fix: In `e2_baseline.json`, add `{run_valid: true, classes: {"1": {…, validity_guard_breached: false}, …}}`. Extend the AC4 one-liner: `assert b_top.get('run_valid') is True` and `assert all(not b[k].get('validity_guard_breached') for k in b)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or inline assertion in `run_e2_benchmark.py` that verifies the `review_plan` split-read contract — either cite the specific parameter/flag that separates ticket-store root from code-grounding root, or add a post-run check that the real `.tickets-tracker` branch was not modified and that at least one code-grounding finding was emitted (proving the real checkout was reached).
evidence: 'rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source="local", sign=False, emit_sidecar=False)' — the plan asserts a split-read contract without citing where in __init__.py this separation is enforced or tested

The AC for run_e2_benchmark.py verifies field presence and verdict vocabulary but does NOT verify that the tmp-store isolation actually prevented writes to the real store or that code grounding hit the real checkout
findings.finding: The `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness silently depends on `review_plan(repo_root=<tmp-store>)` routing ALL ticket reads to the tmp-store while code-grounding reads the real in-place checkout — a split-read behavior that is an undocumented coupling assumption about the internal API. If `review_plan` uses `repo_root` for both ticket reads AND code grounding (or neither), the harness either contaminates the real `.tickets-tracker` branch or runs code grounding against stale/empty state, producing silently wrong baseline numbers with no AC catching it.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` paragraph: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
scenarios: If review_plan routes code grounding through repo_root, the harness evaluates plans against an empty synthetic codebase — all capability/grounding criteria fire vacuously, making class-1/4 baseline numbers meaningless
If review_plan ignores repo_root for ticket reads, runs contaminate the real ticket store and REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off alone may not prevent branch writes
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, after the first review_plan call, assert that (a) no new files appeared in the real `.tickets-tracker` path and (b) at least one finding in `e2_verdicts.jsonl` has a criterion from {E4, G1, G2, G6} with non-null `code_ref` pointing to the real checkout — this double-checks the split. Alternatively, add a one-line comment citing the exact __init__.py line/parameter that implements the split.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the `run_e2_benchmark.py` call signature to correctly achieve split-read: either (a) pass `repo_root=<real-checkout>` for code grounding and a separate `tickets_root` override for ticket reads (which requires using the attested-mode ContextVar path or a dedicated harness parameter), or (b) use `source='attested'` with a pre-populated pinned ticket snapshot — and add an AC assertion that code-grounding criteria fire against real source files (e.g. a known file exists at `ctx.repo_root`).
evidence: `src/rebar/llm/gate_source.py:104-117`: `local` mode in `gate_read_root` does NOT invoke `use_tickets_root` or `use_code_root` — no ContextVar is set
`src/rebar/llm/config.py:97-98`: `resolve_code_root` cascade step 1: `if repo_root: return str(repo_root)` — a truthy `repo_root=<tmp-store>` immediately wins, routing code grounding to the tmp-store
`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:195-203`: `PlanContext.repo_root` is set via `resolve_code_root(repo_root, ...)` — with `repo_root=<tmp-store>` this yields the tmp-store as the code root

ABSENCE: no attested-mode materialize step is described for code grounding in the benchmark — the plan relies entirely on `source=local` to read the real checkout, but the `repo_root` parameter overrides that
Partially-verified: the ticket-read half IS correct (context assembly uses `repo_root` for `show_ticket`/`list_tickets` calls directly); the code-grounding half is Contradicted
findings.finding: The plan asserts `review_plan(id, repo_root=<tmp-store>, source="local", ...)` provides a split-read where 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout' — but this is architecturally contradicted by the source. In `local` mode, `gate_read_root` sets NO ContextVars; `resolve_code_root(repo_root=<tmp-store>, ...)` returns `str(<tmp-store>)` at cascade step 1 (the truthy `repo_root` wins immediately), routing code grounding to the empty tmp-store, not the real checkout. Criteria E4, G1G2, G6 — which ground against real source files — would evaluate against the synthetic codebase, silently producing vacuous results for classes 1 and 4 (the classes R1 and R4 depend on).
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
scenarios: Class 1 (absent asserted capability) and class 4 (unnecessary mechanism) rely on code-grounding criteria E4, G1G2, G6. With code grounding hitting the empty tmp-store, these criteria either abstain (no_repo_root) or vacuously pass — the baseline detection rates for R1 and R4's reference corpus are silently wrong.
The INDETERMINATE run-validity guard (>20% abstain triggers re-run) may be triggered by the empty codebase causing widespread P2/no_repo_root abstains, masking the real issue.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a covering AC that verifies: if any corpus row has `synthetic_base: true`, the e2_baseline.json table includes a separate `synthetic_base_count` (or equivalent) per class, and the README section notes the real-vs-synthetic split.
evidence: "synthesize construct-valid base plans for THAT class only, label them `synthetic_base: true`, and report those numbers separately from the real-base classes so reduced ecological validity is visible"
AC1 verify script only counts mutant rows per class; it does not check for `synthetic_base` field or separate real/synthetic counts
No AC covers the README or baseline reporting of real-vs-synthetic separation
findings.finding: The degradation ladder describes reporting synthetic-base mutants separately (`synthetic_base: true`, "report those numbers separately from the real-base classes") but neither AC1 (corpus schema check) nor any other AC verifies that (a) the field exists on synthetic rows or (b) the baseline table distinguishes real-base vs synthetic-base detection rates if the ladder is invoked.
findings.location: ## What (Degradation if a class cannot reach 10 real base plans) vs ACs
scenarios: If classes 2 or 3 fall back to synthetic bases, the `synthetic_base` flag is missing from committed rows and the baseline table mixes real and synthetic numbers — reduced ecological validity becomes invisible to R3 consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the `test_e2_counting.py` AC to explicitly require test cases for: (a) a verdict dict whose top-level verdict is PASS but contains a target-family advisory finding (must be detected=True), (b) a verdict dict with a block finding on a NON-target criterion (must not increment blocking-FP), and (c) a verdict dict mixing target-advisory and non-target-block findings — so the finding-level `decision` + `target_criteria` filter logic is directly covered, not just the top-level verdict state.
evidence: AC says: 'covered by a golden test with synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' — names verdict-level states, not finding-level `decision` variants

Counting rule specifies: 'detected: a target-family finding surfaced with decision ∈ {block, advisory}' and 'blocking-FP: decision == block on the unmutated original' — the target-criteria filter and advisory-counts distinction are per-finding, not per-verdict

A test that only varies top-level verdict would miss: advisory finding on target criterion (should be detected=True), block finding on non-target criterion (should not be blocking-FP=True), verdict with both target-advisory and non-target-block findings
findings.finding: The unit-test AC for `classify_pair` describes test cases by top-level verdict state ("synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts") without requiring coverage of the finding-level `decision` field logic — specifically: (a) a target-family finding with `decision=advisory` must count as detected (not just BLOCK-verdict cases), (b) a `decision=block` finding on a NON-target criterion must NOT count as blocking-FP, and (c) a mix of target and non-target findings in one verdict dict. These are the subtle, most-bug-prone branches of the counting rule and are not named in the AC.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — fifth AC bullet (`test_e2_counting.py`); also `## What` AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE section, `classify_pair` spec
scenarios: Advisory-only detection: gate returns PASS-verdict but advisory finding on target criterion → should count as detected; test misses this → recall undercounted
Non-target block: gate returns BLOCK on a non-target criterion for the control → classify_pair incorrectly counts it as blocking-FP → FP rate inflated → classes fail thresholds spuriously
Mixed findings: verdict_dict has one target-advisory and one non-target-block finding → correct classification requires both rules; untested
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC bullet for `test_e2_counting.py`, replace 'synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' with: 'synthetic verdict dicts covering: (i) top-level PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE, (ii) target-family advisory finding on a PASS-verdict mutant (detected=True), (iii) block finding on non-target criterion on a PASS-verdict control (blocking_fp=False), (iv) INDETERMINATE verdict (detected=False, blocking_fp=False), and (v) mixed target+non-target findings in one dict.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC5 test specification to explicitly require golden cases covering: (a) PASS-verdict with target-criterion advisory finding (→ detected=True), (b) BLOCK-verdict on non-target criterion for a control (→ blocking_fp=False), and (c) mixed finding dict with both target-advisory and non-target-block findings — so the per-finding `decision` filter logic is contractually covered.
evidence: Counting rule in ## What: 'detected (mutant, recall): a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}` — advisory counts, because the proposed criteria ship advisory-first'
Counting rule in ## What: 'blocking-FP (control): a target-family finding surfaced with `decision == block` on the unmutated original — advisory findings on controls are NOT false positives'
AC5 test description: 'covered by a golden test with synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' — specifies top-level verdict states only

The distinction between advisory-count-as-detected and block-only-as-FP is a per-finding rule, not a per-verdict rule; a verdict dict can be PASS with an advisory finding on a target criterion (→ detected=True) or BLOCK on a non-target criterion (→ blocking_fp=False for that control)
The live plan_review_verdict schema confirms findings carry a `decision` field (sidecar.py:429, orchestrator.py:404,417)
findings.finding: The `classify_pair` counting rule critically depends on per-finding `decision` field values (`block`, `advisory`, `dropped`, `indeterminate`), but the AC5 test description only requires 'synthetic PASS/BLOCK/INDETERMINATE verdict dicts' — top-level verdict states — leaving the most bug-prone branches of the decision protocol untested by specification.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (`test_e2_counting.py`) vs. ## What — AMBIGUOUS/INDETERMINATE counting rule (`classify_pair` spec)
scenarios: Advisory-only detection: gate returns top-level PASS verdict but emits an advisory finding on a target criterion; per the counting rule this should be detected=True; a test that only varies top-level verdict misses this → recall undercounted

Non-target block: gate returns BLOCK on a non-target criterion for the control; per the counting rule blocking_fp should be False; a top-level-verdict-only test would incorrectly count this as a blocking FP → FP rate inflated → classes spuriously fail thresholds

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 proving command to assert all required keys are present in every row (e.g. add `required_m={'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children','mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; required_c={'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children'}; assert all(required_m<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant') and all(required_c<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='control'), 'missing fields'`).
evidence: 'every row carrying `pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and (mutants) a `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`'
The one-liner: `m=c.Counter(r['defect_class'] for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant')` — only counts by class, never inspects field keys
findings.finding: AC1's proving command counts rows by defect_class and role but does NOT verify that every row contains all required fields (`pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and for mutants `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`). A corpus with rows missing `mutation_diff` or `children` would pass the proving command yet violate the AC's stated requirement.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — first AC (corpus shape check), proving command
scenarios: assemble_e2_mutants.py omits `mutation_diff` for class-1 mutants; proving command still prints 'OK'
A mutant row with `children: null` passes the count check; downstream harness crashes on `None`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the one-liner with a version that also checks: `required_m={'pair_id','defect_class','role','source_ticket_id','plan_text','children','mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; required_c=required_m-{'mutation_op','mutation_diff'}; assert all(required_m<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant') and all(required_c<=set(r) for r in rows if r['role']=='control'), 'field schema violation'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1's verify script to assert that every row with `role=='mutant'` contains both `mutation_op` and `mutation_diff` keys (e.g., add `assert all('mutation_op' in r and 'mutation_diff' in r for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant')`).
evidence: AC1 text: "every row carrying `pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role ∈ {mutant,control}`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and (mutants) a `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`"
AC1 prove script: `m=c.Counter(r['defect_class'] for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant')` — counts mutants but does not check `'mutation_op' in r and 'mutation_diff' in r` for mutant rows
findings.finding: AC1's verify script checks mutant counts per class but does NOT verify that mutant rows actually carry the mutant-specific fields `mutation_op` and `mutation_diff`. The script only checks field presence for ALL rows via the outer key list, and the assertion `m[k]>=10 and m[k]==ctl[k]` only counts rows — a mutant row missing `mutation_op`/`mutation_diff` would pass AC1 silently.
findings.location: AC1 prove step (the python3 -c one-liner)
scenarios: assemble_e2_mutants.py writes mutant rows without mutation_diff (e.g., stores the diff inline in mutation_op); AC1 passes; downstream diffing for audit fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the existing one-liner: `assert all('mutation_op' in r and 'mutation_diff' in r for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'), 'mutant missing mutation_op/diff'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cross-check step to AC4's proving command that loads `e2_thresholds.json` and asserts `meets_threshold` equals `detection_rate >= detection_min and fp_rate <= blocking_fp_max` for every class.
evidence: `req={'n_mutants','n_controls','detected','detection_rate','blocking_fp','fp_rate','indeterminate_mutants','indeterminate_controls','meets_threshold'}; assert set(b)=={'1','2','3','4','5'} and all(req<=set(b[k]) for k in b)`
This asserts key presence only; correctness of `meets_threshold` relative to `e2_thresholds.json` is not asserted
findings.finding: AC4's proving command verifies that the `meets_threshold` field EXISTS in `e2_baseline.json` but does NOT verify it is correctly computed from the pre-registered thresholds — a harness bug that sets all `meets_threshold` values to `false` (or hardcodes `true`) would pass AC4's check. The 'meets_threshold' field is the key signal consumed by R1/R3/R4, so its correctness is material.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — fourth AC (baseline run), proving command
scenarios: A harness bug sets `meets_threshold=True` for all classes regardless of measured rates; AC4 passes; R1/R3/R4 consume incorrect baseline
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC4 one-liner: `import json; t=json.load(open('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e2_thresholds.json')); [assert b[k]['meets_threshold'] == (b[k]['detection_rate'] >= t['classes'][k]['detection_min'] and b[k]['fp_rate'] <= t['classes'][k]['blocking_fp_max']), f'meets_threshold wrong for class {k}' for k in b]`

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean, good
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanical algorithm for the class-1 mutation in `assemble_e2_mutants.py`'s spec — e.g., 'scan the base plan text for explicit capability claims (regex on phrases like "X provides / X supports / X handles"); look up the named module in the repo; if the claim is already true, negate it by substituting a named method that does not exist; record the substituted phrase as `mutation_diff`' — so the executor can implement it without judgment calls.
evidence: 'plan asserts an existing named module provides a capability it lacks'

Classes 2–5 have unambiguous mechanical operations (re-partition children by layer; delete a depends_on/blocks edge; add a change the plan does not need; strip the [operator-attested] tag), but class 1 gives no equivalent algorithm for how to select the false capability to inject
findings.finding: The class-1 mutation ('absent asserted capability') is described as 'mechanical' but no algorithm is specified for how the executor identifies a capability to falsely assert. The executor must pick a named module from the base plan, then determine a capability that module does NOT provide — which requires code inspection and semantic judgment, not a mechanical text transformation like the other four classes (delete an edge, re-partition children, strip a tag, add an unnecessary step).
findings.location: ## What — defect class 1: 'absent asserted capability'
scenarios: Executor writing assemble_e2_mutants.py cannot implement the class-1 mutation loop without an algorithm: which capability? chosen randomly from some list? hardcoded per ticket? inferred from code reflection?
Two implementors would produce structurally different corpora for class 1, undermining reproducibility
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the class-1 row in ## What or ## Scope: 'Mutation algorithm: (1) regex-scan `plan_text` for phrases matching `<module> (provides|supports|handles|exposes) <capability>`; (2) replace `<capability>` with a plausible-but-absent capability drawn from a hardcoded per-module capability-absence map committed alongside the script (e.g. `capability_absence_map.json`); (3) record the original and replaced phrase as `mutation_diff`.' Alternatively, note that class-1 mutations are hand-authored from the E1-verified MISS cases (dc58-af7b / db7b-c8fd / 5886-d028) and cross-reference the seedpilot harness for the template.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff policy for `review_plan()` API calls in `run_e2_benchmark.py` (e.g., exponential backoff with ≤3 retries on 429/5xx) AND define the crash-resume deduplication rule (e.g., skip a pair whose `pair_id` is already present in `e2_verdicts.jsonl`) so re-runs are idempotent.
evidence: 'runs the CURRENT gate review_plan ... over every mutant + control' — one LLM call per row, ≥100 total
'journals raw verdicts incrementally (crash-resumable)' — recovery mechanism named but deduplication/skip logic not specified
No mention of retry, backoff, or rate-limit sleep anywhere in the plan
T5a checklist: 'Input-size limits, concurrency/rate-limit/pool handling, and load expectations stated'
findings.finding: The benchmark harness makes ≥100 sequential Anthropic API calls (one review_plan() per mutant+control pair) with no specified retry, backoff, or rate-limit handling. A transient 429 or 5xx mid-run will crash the harness and rely entirely on crash-resume, which itself is under-specified (the plan does not say whether the journal is checked to skip already-completed pairs on re-run, so a naïve re-run could duplicate rows in e2_verdicts.jsonl or re-count the same pair twice in e2_baseline.json).
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py description; ## What — 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (crash-resumable)'
scenarios: Anthropic returns HTTP 429 rate-limit at call #47 of 100; harness crashes; re-run without skip logic re-processes calls 1-47, producing duplicate rows in e2_verdicts.jsonl and inflated counts in e2_baseline.json
Transient 5xx on a single review_plan() call propagates as an unhandled exception, losing the in-progress class's partial results despite crash-resume intent
findings.suggested_fix: In run_e2_benchmark.py: (1) wrap each review_plan() call in a retry loop with tenacity or manual exponential backoff (e.g., 3 retries, 2^n seconds, on APIStatusError/RateLimitError); (2) on startup, read existing e2_verdicts.jsonl to build a set of completed pair_ids and skip those pairs — making the run fully idempotent under re-execution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanical algorithm for the class-1 mutation in the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` spec — e.g., 'locate explicit capability claims in the base plan text via regex (phrases like "X provides / X supports / X handles Y"); resolve the named module in the repo; verify the claimed method/function does not exist; substitute the claimed capability with a plausible-but-absent one; record the substituted phrase as `mutation_diff`'.
evidence: Class 1 definition: 'plan asserts an existing named module provides a capability it lacks (the E1-verified MISS class)'

Classes 2–5 each have unambiguous mechanical operations: 'children re-partitioned by architectural layer'; 'NO `depends_on`/`blocks` edge'; 'a change the plan does not need'; '`[operator-attested]` tag stripped'
The plan provides no equivalent algorithm for class 1: which module to pick, which capability to falsely claim, how to phrase the claim, or how to verify the module does NOT provide that capability
The E1 corpus references (dc58-af7b / db7b-c8fd / 5886-d028) are labeled as examples but no extraction algorithm is given
findings.finding: The class-1 mutation ('absent asserted capability') is described as 'mechanical' but the plan provides no algorithm for how `assemble_e2_mutants.py` should select or construct the false capability claim — unlike classes 2–5 which have unambiguous mechanical operations (re-partition children by layer; delete an edge; add an unneeded step; strip a tag).
findings.location: ## What — defect class 1: 'absent asserted capability'; ## Scope — `assemble_e2_mutants.py`
scenarios: Implementor of `assemble_e2_mutants.py` must make arbitrary choices about which capability to falsely inject, producing class-1 mutants of inconsistent difficulty (some trivially detectable, some not)
Without a deterministic algorithm, re-running the script produces a different class-1 corpus, undermining pre-registration reproducibility

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the README.md AC grep (or the README spec in ## Scope) to verify the counting-rule contract is explicitly stated: add `grep -q "advisory" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md` (or equivalent) so the counting rule — advisory=detected, INDETERMINATE=FN, 20% cap triggers re-run — is confirmed present in the public-facing writeup, not just in ## What of this plan.
evidence: AC grep: `grep -q "run-validity" ... && grep -Eq "degrad" ... && echo WRITEUP-OK` — no grep for 'advisory', 'INDETERMINATE', or 'classify_pair'

## What documents the counting rule in detail but the README spec ('recording the baseline results, the pre-registered thresholds, how R1/R3/R4/R2 each consume the corpus, the INDETERMINATE run-validity guard, and the degradation ladder') omits explicit mention of the advisory-counts-for-detection rule
findings.finding: The README.md acceptance criterion only greps for `run-validity` and `degrad` keywords — it does not verify that the counting-rule contract (advisory findings count as detected, INDETERMINATE → FN on mutants / non-FP on controls, 20% INDETERMINATE cap triggers re-run) is captured in the README. Since R1, R3, R4, and R2 all consume E2's detection/FP numbers, a future story author who reads the README but not ## What of this plan could misinterpret advisory-only flags as non-detections or treat INDETERMINATE runs as valid.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — sixth AC (`README.md` grep prove step)
scenarios: A future R1/R3/R4 story author reads the README, sees detection_rate numbers, and doesn't realise advisory findings are included — re-implements a stricter 'block-only' counting rule, producing incompatible discrimination numbers
findings.suggested_fix: Add one grep to the sixth AC: `&& grep -q "advisory" docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md` after the existing greps, and add a bullet to the README spec list: 'the counting rule (advisory findings count as detected; INDETERMINATE ⇒ FN on mutants, non-FP on controls; >20% INDETERMINATE invalidates the run).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `classify_pair` input schema to `## Scope` or `e2_counting.py` description specifying the `verdict_dict` structure (e.g., `{verdict: str, blocking: [{criteria: [str], decision: str}], advisory: [{criteria: [str], decision: str}], indeterminate: [{criteria: [str]}]}`  ) and the `target_criteria` type (e.g., `set[str]`).
evidence: Plan states: 'A pure helper `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` applies' the counting rule — no input schema specified

The counting rule references 'a target-family finding surfaced with `decision ∈ {block, advisory}`' — implying `verdict_dict` has iterable findings with `criteria` and `decision` keys — but this structure is never spelled out in the plan
`plan_review_verdict.schema.json` declares `blocking` and `advisory` as `array` of `object` with `additionalProperties: true` — permissive, no required fields on finding items
The `e2_counting.py` is specified as having 'no `anthropic`/LLM import' but its accepted data contract is unspecified
findings.finding: The `verdict_dict` input schema accepted by `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` is never defined in the plan — the required keys, nesting structure (`blocking[]`, `advisory[]`, each item's `criteria` and `decision` fields) are only inferrable from the live `plan_review_verdict.schema.json`, which is declared `additionalProperties: true` and has no strict enum on `decision`.
findings.location: ## Scope — `e2_counting.py` description; ## What — `classify_pair` counting rule; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: Implementor structures `verdict_dict` as `{verdict: 'BLOCK', blocking: [{criteria: ['G3'], decision: 'block'}]}` while the harness passes `{verdict: 'BLOCK', findings: [...]}` — `classify_pair` silently returns wrong results
The `target_criteria` parameter type is unspecified — list of strings? set? dict? — leading to inconsistent implementations between `classify_pair` and its callers

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the sub-heading to "INDETERMINATE verdict handling" (drop "AMBIGUOUS /") or explicitly note that 'AMBIGUOUS' here refers to seedpilot.py's vocabulary (distinct from the production gate) to prevent implementer confusion.
evidence: Sub-heading: "AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling (the counting rule, pinned)"
Body: "The production gate emits verdict `PASS | BLOCK | INDETERMINATE`"
"AMBIGUOUS" does not appear in the gate vocabulary or anywhere else in the plan as a verdict value
findings.finding: The ## What sub-heading reads "AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling" but the plan body under it exclusively uses INDETERMINATE (the actual gate vocabulary: `PASS | BLOCK | INDETERMINATE`). "AMBIGUOUS" appears nowhere else in the gate vocabulary and is not defined; the heading introduces a term that is inconsistent with the described system.
findings.location: ## What, sub-heading "AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling"
scenarios: An implementer reading the sub-heading may attempt to handle an AMBIGUOUS verdict case that doesn't exist in the production gate, or conflate it with seedpilot.py's AMBIGUOUS verdict (a different system).
findings.suggested_fix: Change sub-heading to: "INDETERMINATE verdict handling (the counting rule, pinned)"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed `mutation_op` vocabulary in ## What or AC1 — e.g., `{'absent_asserted_capability', 'layer_cake_split', 'missing_ordering_edge', 'unnecessary_mechanism', 'stripped_operator_tag'}` — and add a prove-step assertion that every mutant row's `mutation_op` is in this set.
evidence: AC1 states every mutant row must carry `mutation_op` as a required field: 'every row carrying `pair_id`, `defect_class`, `role ∈ {mutant,control}`, `source_ticket_id`, `plan_text`, `children`, and (mutants) a `mutation_op` + `mutation_diff`'

The plan describes five defect classes (absent capability, layer-cake split, consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge, unnecessary mechanism, untagged operational AC) but never names what string value `mutation_op` should carry for each class
No enum, no mapping table, no example values for `mutation_op` appear anywhere in ## What, ## Scope, or ## Acceptance Criteria
The AC1 prove script only asserts cardinality (`m[k]>=10`) and key presence, not `mutation_op` vocabulary correctness
findings.finding: `mutation_op` is required in every mutant row (AC1 schema) but its value vocabulary is never defined anywhere in the plan — not as an enum, not as a closed set of strings, not as a mapping from class number to operation name.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC1 corpus schema (`mutation_op` field) and ## What — defect class descriptions
scenarios: Two implementors independently choose different strings for `mutation_op` (e.g., 'class_1' vs. 'absent_capability' vs. 'absent-asserted-capability'); downstream consumers of the corpus (R1, R3, R4) that filter by `mutation_op` break
The `assemble_e2_mutants.py` script author and the `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness author disagree on the vocabulary, producing a corpus that fails downstream joins
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1's prove script: `OPS={'absent_asserted_capability','layer_cake_split','missing_ordering_edge','unnecessary_mechanism','stripped_operator_tag'}; assert all(r.get('mutation_op') in OPS for r in rows if r['role']=='mutant'), 'bad mutation_op'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup path for the throwaway rebar temp store in `run_e2_benchmark.py` — use a `try/finally` block or `contextlib.ExitStack` with `shutil.rmtree(tmp_dir, ignore_errors=True)` so the temp directory is deleted whether the run completes or crashes.
evidence: 'rebar init under tempfile.mkdtemp(), REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off' — creation is specified
No mention of shutil.rmtree, atexit, contextlib.ExitStack, or any cleanup in the plan
T9 checklist: 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate'
T5b checklist: 'Recovery without data loss/corruption; writes idempotent; partial state safe/durable'
findings.finding: The throwaway rebar store created via `tempfile.mkdtemp()` has no specified RETIRE/cleanup path. Repeated runs or crashes will accumulate orphaned temp directories containing materialized ticket data on disk, with no mechanism to discover or remove them.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py: 'ISOLATED throwaway rebar store (rebar init under tempfile.mkdtemp())'
scenarios: Harness crashes after creating the temp store but before completing the run; the orphaned directory with materialized ticket data persists indefinitely
Benchmark is run multiple times during development; each run leaves a separate mkdtemp directory; disk accumulates gigabytes of throwaway ticket stores
A security-conscious environment flags unmanaged temp directories containing (possibly sensitive) plan text extracted from the corpus
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the temp store in a context manager: `with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmp_dir:` — this guarantees cleanup on both normal exit and exceptions, replacing the manual mkdtemp() call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s throwaway-store bootstrap can call `rebar.init_repo(repo_root=d)` directly (as `eval_solver.case_store()` does) rather than shelling out `rebar init`, and document why a custom scaffold is needed if corpus-materialization requires steps `case_store()` does not cover.
evidence: `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py:55-63`: `@contextmanager def case_store(case): ... with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory(prefix='rebar-eval-') as d: _git_init(d); rebar.init_repo(repo_root=d); ...` — production code for the same pattern

`eval_solver.case_store()` writes fixture files from a case dict; the benchmark's use-case is materializing ticket-graph rows from a corpus, which is functionally different but the git-init + rebar.init_repo bootstrap portion is identical
ABSENCE: plan gives no rationale for not reusing the bootstrap steps from `eval_solver.case_store()`
findings.finding: The `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness plans to bootstrap a throwaway rebar store via `rebar init` under `tempfile.mkdtemp()`, but the production codebase already provides `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.case_store()` which does exactly this (`_git_init(d)` + `rebar.init_repo(repo_root=d)` under `TemporaryDirectory`). The plan does not confirm whether the existing helper is reusable or explain why the benchmark needs its own re-implementation of the store-bootstrap scaffold.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description: 'Materializes each mutant AND its control ... as tickets in an ISOLATED throwaway rebar store (`rebar init` under `tempfile.mkdtemp()`, `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`...)'
scenarios: The harness re-implements git-init + rebar.init_repo in a slightly different way (e.g., missing a step `eval_solver` handles), causing store-init failures during benchmark runs.
A future change to rebar store bootstrap conventions must be updated in two places.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace any shell-out `rebar init` with `import rebar; rebar.init_repo(repo_root=tmp_dir)` (matching `eval_solver.case_store()`'s approach), then add any corpus-specific ticket-materialization steps on top. If `case_store()` itself is close enough, import and adapt it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in ## Scope (or the AC) that `tests/unit/experiments/` must be created as a new directory (with `__init__.py` if the project uses package-style test layout), since it does not currently exist.
evidence: Directory listing of `tests/unit/` shows no `experiments/` subdirectory.
pytest `testpaths = ["tests"]` will discover the file IF the directory exists, but the directory must be created and the plan is silent on this.
The AC proof command references the path directly without any `mkdir` step.
findings.finding: The test file `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` is placed in a subdirectory `tests/unit/experiments/` that does not currently exist in the repo. The plan does not mention creating this directory or any required `__init__.py`/conftest, yet the AC proof command invokes it directly.
findings.location: ## Scope — `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py` / AC line 5 (`.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -q`)
scenarios: Implementor delivers only the `.py` file without the parent directory, causing `pytest` to fail with a collection error on the AC proof command.
If `tests/unit/experiments/` lacks an `__init__.py` and the repo uses namespace-package style tests, pytest may or may not collect it depending on `rootdir` config — the plan gives no guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/experiments/__init__.py` to the deliverables list in ## Scope, mirroring the pattern used by other `tests/unit/` subdirectories.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or address the `ctx.tickets_root=None` issue in local mode: either (a) have `run_e2_benchmark.py` use `attested` mode with the tmp store as the tickets snapshot, (b) set `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp-store>/.tickets-tracker` for the duration of each `review_plan` call to force all ticket reads to the isolated store, or (c) document that sidecar-history reads hitting the live store is an accepted limitation and quantify the expected noise.
evidence: Plan: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
Code: `gate_read_root` (gate_source.py:104-117) — local mode block is bare `yield` with no `use_tickets_root` call
Code: `orchestrator._assemble_context_uncached` line 208: `tickets_root=current_tickets_root()` — returns `None` in local mode
Code: `pass1.py:504`: `concerns = sidecar.prior_concerns(ctx.ticket_id, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` — resolves to `tracker_dir(None)` = live store
findings.finding: In `source="local"` mode, `ctx.tickets_root` is `None` because `gate_read_root` only calls `use_tickets_root` in attested mode (local mode yields without setting the ContextVar). As a result, intra-review cross-ticket reads in Pass-1 (e.g., `sidecar.prior_concerns(ctx.ticket_id, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` at `pass1.py:504`) and agentic-tier rebar reads resolve against `tracker_dir(None)` — the live checkout's `.tickets-tracker`, not the tmp store. This means the isolation guarantee stated in the plan ('ticket reads hit the tmp store') is incomplete: only the primary `show_ticket` / `list_tickets` calls (which receive `repo_root=<tmp-store>` via `assemble_context`) are isolated; sub-reads that use `ctx.tickets_root` (which is `None`) fall through to the live store.
findings.location: ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py`: 'ticket reads hit the tmp store, code grounding reads the real in-place checkout'
scenarios: A mutant ticket ID happens to collide with a live ticket's ID in the real store: `prior_concerns` returns live findings, contaminating the sidecar-history input to Pass-1 and producing spurious advisory findings on controls.
The agentic tier's tool calls that use `ctx.tickets_root` fetch live ticket descriptions instead of synthetic mutant descriptions, producing inconsistent review inputs and non-reproducible baselines.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference and/or reuse `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.case_store()` (and its git-init + `rebar.init_repo` sequence) as the established in-repo pattern for ephemeral isolated rebar stores, rather than re-specifying the `rebar init` + `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` bootstrap from scratch.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py:56-63: case_store() context manager does git init + rebar.init_repo(repo_root=d) in a TemporaryDirectory — the exact pattern the plan describes, already implemented

src/rebar/opcert_service/workspace.py:47-105: prepare_workspace() shows the full init sequence needed for an isolated store (git init, worktree add for tickets branch, run_ensures for .env-id convergence, remote removal)
Plan does not reference eval_solver.case_store() or workspace.py as prior art for the harness's store-bootstrapping approach
findings.finding: The plan re-invents an isolated ephemeral rebar store pattern (`rebar init under tempfile.mkdtemp(), REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`) that already exists in-repo as `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.case_store()` (which does `rebar.init_repo(repo_root=d)` in a `tempfile.TemporaryDirectory`). Not considering this prior art risks diverging from the established pattern and missing initialization steps that `case_store` handles (e.g., git init, .env-id seeding, ensures convergence).
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py: 'rebar init under tempfile.mkdtemp(), REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off so nothing touches the shared .tickets-tracker branch'
scenarios: If run_e2_benchmark.py's store init omits steps that case_store handles (e.g., the .env-id marker rebar requires, the ensures pass), ticket writes may silently fail or produce malformed stores, causing cryptic errors during the benchmark run

findings.finding: AC3 requires 'the benchmark harness' to run the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and record a baseline table, but the Scope section lists only 'a mutation-assembly script' — the harness is a distinct deliverable that has no corresponding step, meaning AC3 has no described step that reaches it.
findings.location: Scope section ('A mutation-assembly script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/.') vs AC3 ('The benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the baseline detection/FP table per class').
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'A benchmark harness script at `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/run_benchmark.sh` that accepts the corpus directory as input, invokes the current gate on each plan, and writes `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/baseline_results.json` with per-class detection/FP counts.'

findings.finding: The plan instructs the executor to pre-register 'the per-class targets in the epic's gate table' for all 5 defect classes before any detector run, but no such single gate table exists: the epic ticket (6982) explicitly states it 'does not duplicate the numbers' — thresholds live in child ACs. More critically, defect class 5 (untagged operational AC) maps to R2 (b080), whose AC specifies lexicon precision/recall on the historical corpus — NOT a seeded-mutant detection threshold against E2 mutants. No per-class detection target for class 5 exists anywhere in the epic's gate structure.
findings.location: What section — 'Pre-register detection thresholds BEFORE running any detector: the per-class targets in the epic's gate table'

findings.finding: The Scope section names only 'A mutation-assembly script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/' as the code artifact to create, but AC3 requires a distinct 'benchmark harness' that runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the detection/FP table per class. These are functionally different scripts (one assembles mutants; one invokes the live gate and collects results), yet only the mutation-assembly script is listed in Scope — the benchmark harness is an unscoped edit target.
findings.location: Scope section ('A mutation-assembly script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/') vs AC3 ('The benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the baseline detection/FP table per class')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Scope line such as: 'A benchmark harness script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/ that invokes the current gate (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/) over the committed mutant+control corpus and writes the per-class detection/FP baseline table.'

findings.finding: AC3 requires 'the benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the baseline detection/FP table per class', but the Scope section names only 'a mutation-assembly script' as a code artifact. No gate-execution harness is described in What or Scope. The mechanism for actually invoking the plan-review gate against the seeded corpus and recording results is entirely absent from the plan's specified deliverables.
findings.location: Scope section ('A mutation-assembly script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/') vs AC3

findings.finding: The plan's benchmark harness must decide whether AMBIGUOUS gate verdicts count as 'detected' (TP on a mutant) or 'not detected' (FN). The gate emits PASS/AMBIGUOUS/FAIL (confirmed in harness.py), and seedpilot.py shows GOOD controls producing AMBIGUOUS at a measurable rate. This counting decision rule is referenced implicitly by 'records the baseline detection/FP table' but is never defined.
findings.location: AC#3: 'records the baseline detection/FP table per class'; What section: 'Pre-register detection thresholds'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the README spec: 'For scoring purposes: on a mutant, FAIL = detected (TP), PASS or AMBIGUOUS = not-detected (FN). On a control, PASS or AMBIGUOUS = not-fired (TN), FAIL = false-positive (FP). Rationale: AMBIGUOUS is only correct when the plan genuinely under-specifies; the gate should FAIL on a mutant with a known defect.'

findings.finding: The 'benchmark harness' that runs the current gate over mutants+controls (AC3) is not described or named in the What section or Scope — only a 'mutation-assembly script' is listed as a Scope deliverable. This leaves AC3 as an orphan criterion with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: Scope / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'A benchmark-runner script (e.g. docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/run_benchmark.sh or equivalent) that invokes the existing gate over each mutant and control, capturing per-class detection and FP counts into a machine-readable results file.' Mirror this in the What section.

findings.finding: The Scope section lists only 'A mutation-assembly script' as a code artifact, but AC3 requires a 'benchmark harness' that runs the current gate over mutants+controls and records a detection/FP table — a distinct, non-trivial artifact not named in Scope. This mismatch means the deliverable list understates what must be built, risking the harness being treated as out-of-scope.
findings.location: Scope section (mutation-assembly script) vs. AC3 (benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet list, add: 'A benchmark-harness script under docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/ that runs the current gate over mutants+controls and writes a detection/FP results table (read-only use of the gate — no gate/source changes).'

findings.finding: AC3's 'records the baseline detection/FP table per class' provides no proving command and no stated output artifact path, making it impossible to verify in-session whether the criterion has been satisfied.
findings.location: AC3: 'The benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the baseline detection/FP table per class (the reference point every R-row's gate is judged against).'

findings.finding: The plan draws mutants from 'real cleanly-closed plans (drawn from the E1 outcome corpus)' but the outcome corpus (outcome_corpus.jsonl) contains ALL 628 reviewed tickets — including those with post-claim description edits (ac-strengthened, substantive-unclassified), force-closes, and reopened tickets. The term 'cleanly-closed' maps to no schema field, no documented filter expression, and no selection procedure anywhere in the corpus tooling or README. An executor has no codebase-grounded definition of which rows qualify.
findings.location: What section — 'Build a seeded-defect benchmark from real cleanly-closed plans (drawn from the E1 outcome corpus)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'Source plans are drawn from outcome corpus rows where `post_claim_edit_class == "none" AND reopen_count == 0 AND force_close == False` — the zero-post-claim-edit population (576 of 628 rows per the corpus README).'

findings.finding: The plan requires recording a 'baseline detection/FP table per class' (AC#3) but never defines the output schema — no field names, file format, or column definitions are specified. The harness executor cannot build this output without a schema.
findings.location: AC#3: 'The benchmark harness runs the CURRENT gate over mutants+controls and records the baseline detection/FP table per class (the reference point every R-row's gate is judged against).'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify e.g.: one JSONL row per (defect_class, mutant_id) with fields `{defect_class, mutant_id, label, verdict, detected: bool, fp: bool}` and a summary CSV with columns `{defect_class, n_mutants, recall, fpr}`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
